title: Drone shows stunning aurora over seaside town
creator: USA TODAY
notes: CANDIDATE-SOURCED, INDEPENDENTLY RE-VERIFIED THIS SESSION -- first exemplar added to the aurora borealis / northern-lights landscapes subgenre bucket, a subject not previously represented in this pack. A prior agent's candidate JSON proposed this URL (https://www.youtube.com/shorts/mYWYXljRbtw); per this task's step 1 requirement it was NOT trusted verbatim -- it was re-verified live in this session via a direct curl against YouTube's oEmbed endpoint (https://www.youtube.com/oembed?url=https://www.youtube.com/shorts/mYWYXljRbtw&format=json), which returned title 'Drone shows stunning aurora over seaside town', author_name 'USA TODAY', author_url 'https://www.youtube.com/@USATODAY', and embed dimensions height=200/width=113 (portrait) -- an exact field-for-field match to the candidate's claimed title, creator, and vertical-Shorts framing, so this session's own primary-source check corroborates rather than merely repeats the prior agent's claim. No Creative Commons marking of any kind was present in the oEmbed response (oEmbed does not surface CC licensing metadata at all, so this is not evidence either way), and the prior agent's attempt to load the watch page directly was blocked by a captcha, so no CC marking could be positively confirmed there either. Per this project's stated default (assume all-rights-reserved unless the uploader's license setting is directly confirmed otherwise), license_category is set to 'all-rights-reserved' -- consistent with USA TODAY being a conventional news-broadcaster channel with standard copyright, not a CC-licensed uploader. Consequently local_media_path stays null and no video file was downloaded or persisted anywhere in this repo. duration is left null: YouTube's oEmbed response does not carry a duration field, and no further verification of runtime (YouTube Data API call or manual playback) was attempted in this session -- rather than fabricate a plausible Shorts-length number, this field stays unset. award_or_showcase_provenance is left null: this is a news-broadcaster repost of a newsworthy weather event (a rare Nov 2025 aurora display visible over a Massachusetts seaside town), not a formally juried contest or festival selection, and no award claim was found anywhere in the oEmbed metadata or the prior agent's research -- inventing one would violate the no-fabrication constraint. Creator-repetition note: this is the second USA TODAY-credited exemplar in this pack (alongside usa-today-sand-dune-surfing.json). This session did not independently re-litigate that tradeoff, but notes the pack already has precedent for a repeat creator (THE NATURE PROJECT appears twice, in the-nature-project-horseshoe-bend.json and the-nature-project-salar-de-uyuni.json), and no other candidate covering this rare astro-landscape subgenre was identified at comparable confidence, so the repetition is treated as an acceptable tradeoff for subject-matter distinctiveness rather than a redundant pick. scores (sharpness/exposure/motion_smoothness) are scores_provenance: manually_estimated placeholder judgments, NOT measurements from Capability 1's actual scoring functions (scoring_sharpness.py/scoring_exposure.py/scoring_motion_smoothness.py) -- no source file was ever downloaded to run those functions against, per the spec's no-bulk-download constraint. Calibration reasoning, consistent with this pack's existing ~0.70-0.88 range: sharpness=0.70 (this is a low-light/night aerial capture of a diffuse, faint natural light phenomenon -- aurora glow is considerably fainter and lower-contrast than the point-source artificial lighting in this pack's other night exemplar, skywave-city-skyline-blue-hour-night.json (sharpness 0.72), which plausibly requires higher sensor gain/ISO and therefore more perceptible noise and softer apparent detail at the same nominal resolution, so scored a shade below that comparable night-capture precedent); exposure=0.69 (an aurora-over-town night scene is an extreme dynamic-range case -- a very dim, diffuse celestial light source in a dark sky, set against a foreground of bright artificial streetlights/building lights from the seaside town, is harder to expose evenly than either a plain lit skyline (skywave-city-skyline-blue-hour-night.json, exposure 0.71) or a daylight scene, and no HDR/log-profile or bracketed-exposure claim was found in the verified metadata to indicate this was handled better than a typical single-exposure video capture, so scored at the low end of this pack's range, just below that comparable night exemplar); motion_smoothness=0.75 (the genre and title -- a 'stunning' reveal of a static overhead natural-light scene -- is consistent with a slow, deliberate hover or gentle pan rather than an active tracking shot, since capturing faint aurora light in video without motion blur/streaking plausibly requires minimizing camera movement; scored above this pack's active-tracking exemplars such as usa-today-sand-dune-surfing.json's 0.72 pursuit shot, but below its confirmed single continuous stabilized reveals such as skywave-city-skyline-blue-hour-night.json's 0.80, since no explicit gimbal-stabilization-software claim, e.g. ReelSteady/Gyroflow, was found for this specific clip, and low-light video capture is a plausible, if undocumented, source of additional perceived jitter/rolling-shutter artifacts). composite_score IS a real computation: it was produced this session by calling this repo's own drone_video_ai.highlight_extraction.composite.compute_composite_score with these three manually-estimated inputs (sharpness=0.70, exposure=0.69, motion_smoothness=0.75, composition=None) and drone_video_ai.highlight_extraction.weights.default_weights() via '.venv/bin/activate' then python3 in this session. default_weights() with no explicit version argument returned weights_version 'default-v2' (sharpness=exposure=motion_smoothness=composition=0.25 each); because composition's score is None (this pack's Milestone-1 Capability-1 convention), compute_composite_score's defensive None-skipping behavior excludes composition from both the weighted sum and the renormalization denominator, so the result reduces to the equal-weighted average of the three present signals: (0.70+0.69+0.75)/3 = 0.7133333333333333, confirmed by actually running the function this session rather than hand-computed. Rounded to 0.7133 here. editorial_style: this session first attempted a live playback review via browser automation, per this task's process, but was blocked by browser-profile-lock errors on both available automation tools -- Playwright's browser_navigate returned 'Browser is already in use ... use --isolated' on two attempts, and chrome-devtools-mcp's new_page/list_pages returned an equivalent 'browser already running for chrome-profile ... use --isolated' error on two attempts, both consistent with a concurrent session holding the shared browser profile rather than a fixable navigation/login/ad issue; claude-in-chrome was also tried first and reported 'Browser extension is not connected'. Per this task's instruction to stop after 3-4 attempts on profile-lock rather than retry indefinitely, playback review was abandoned after 4 total attempts and the session fell back to text-provenance research. format='short-form-reel' is grounded in structural facts obtained via this session's own oEmbed fetch, not a guess from the title/branding alone -- (1) the source URL itself is a native /shorts/ path, YouTube's own platform-level designation for short-form vertical content; (2) the oEmbed response's embed dimensions (height=200, width=113) describe a portrait/vertical aspect ratio -- the same structural-evidence basis this pack already uses in abc-news-volcano-crater-lava-fpv.json (also a native /shorts/ URL with identical 200x113 portrait dimensions), and the deliberate opposite/counter-example of usa-today-sand-dune-surfing.json (a /watch?v= URL with landscape 113x200 dimensions, where format was correctly left null). A direct WebFetch of the /shorts/ watch page in this session returned only the title, with the rest of the page body truncated/blocked (consistent with YouTube bot-detection on direct scrape of watch pages); a WebFetch of the Instagram companion-reel URL cited in the prior agent's verification_notes returned unrelated content (a Denver 'Mile High Drone Shows' promotional reel, not this aurora video, likely an Instagram anti-bot/generic-feed substitution for an unauthenticated fetch), so it was not used as evidence; and WebSearch queries surfaced only this same YouTube Shorts URL plus generic Massachusetts-drone-sightings/aurora news coverage unrelated to editing technique. No press coverage or creator commentary describing cut count, shot length, or transition style was found in any source consulted, so estimated_cut_count, avg_shot_length_seconds, and transition_styles_observed stay null/empty rather than guessed -- a punchy single-subject news short of a stationary aurora/seaside scene plausibly involves few internal cuts, but that is a genre inference, not an observation, and is not reported as a specific number.

editorial_style.pacing_notes: Live playback review was attempted but blocked by browser-profile-lock issues on both available automation tools: Playwright (browser_navigate) returned 'Browser is already in use ... use --isolated' on two attempts, and chrome-devtools-mcp (new_page / list_pages) returned an equivalent 'browser already running for chrome-profile ... use --isolated' error on two attempts -- both consistent with another concurrent session already holding the shared browser profile, not a fixable navigation/login/ad issue, so per this task's instruction to stop after 3-4 attempts on profile-lock rather than retry indefinitely, playback review was abandoned after 4 total attempts across the two tools (claude-in-chrome was also tried first and reported 'Browser extension is not connected'). Fell back to text-provenance research: a direct WebFetch of the /shorts/ watch page returned only the title, with the rest of the page body truncated/blocked (consistent with YouTube bot-detection on direct scrape of watch pages); a WebFetch of the Instagram companion-reel URL cited in the prior agent's verification_notes returned unrelated content (a Denver 'Mile High Drone Shows' promotional reel, not this aurora video -- likely an Instagram anti-bot/generic-feed substitution for an unauthenticated fetch, so not used as evidence); and two WebSearch queries (creator+subject, and exact title string) surfaced only this same YouTube Shorts URL plus generic Massachusetts-drone-sightings/aurora news coverage unrelated to editing technique -- no press coverage or creator commentary describing cut count, shot length, or transition style was found anywhere. This session independently ran its own YouTube oEmbed fetch (https://www.youtube.com/oembed?url=https://www.youtube.com/shorts/mYWYXljRbtw&format=json), which returned title='Drone shows stunning aurora over seaside town', author_name='USA TODAY', author_url='https://www.youtube.com/@USATODAY', and vertical embed dimensions height=200/width=113 on the native /shorts/ URL path -- corroborating the prior agent's oEmbed finding and matching this pack's abc-news-volcano-crater-lava-fpv.json precedent (identical structural signature: native /shorts/ path + portrait 200x113 oEmbed dimensions treated as basis to assert format from structure, not from the title/branding alone; this is also the opposite, confirming pattern to this pack's usa-today-sand-dune-surfing.json counter-example, where a /watch?v= URL with landscape 113x200 oEmbed dimensions correctly kept format null). On that basis format is set to 'short-form-reel', but no cut-count, shot-length, or transition-style detail was found in any source consulted, so those fields stay null/empty rather than guessed -- a punchy single-subject news short of a stationary aurora/seaside scene plausibly involves few internal cuts, but that is a genre inference, not an observation, and is not reported as a specific number.
